parts.answer:  correct balanced equation Example of correct equation: (NH ) CO → 2NH + H O + CO Allow multiples H CO for H O + CO Ignore state symbols even if incorrect (1) Number Additional guidance Mar k

For ammonia  test: reaction with hydrogen chloride / HCl(g) (1)  result: white smoke (1) For water  test: add (anhydrous) copper((II)) sulfate or cobalt((II)) chloride (1)  result: white to blue or blue to pink (1) For carbon dioxide  test: (add / add to) lime water or (saturated) solution of calcium hydroxide (1)  result: any indication that a white suspension is formed (1) For all the tests ignore indicators If name and formula given both must be correct Observation marks are dependent on test Allow (add /introduce / place next to) HCl If HCl(aq) / conc HCl is used a suitable method is needed e.g. dipping a glass rod into HCl(aq) or opening a bottle of HCl(aq) close to the ammonia. Do not award ‘add hydrochloric acid’ / HCl(aq) / other hydrogen halides but allow the result mark Allow white fumes / white solid Do not award steamy / misty fumes / precipitate /cloud Accept CuSO / CoCl If start & finish colours are given both must be correct Allow just CuSO turns blue or CoCl turns pink Allow observation mark if CuSO / CoCl solutions are used Do not award CoCl turns red Ignore boiling temperature measurement Accept Ca(OH) ((aq)) turns cloudy / turns milky / white precipitate forms (6) number Additional guidance Mark

Diagram showing collecting test tube angled down with mouth of the tube close to and below that of the heated test tube Example of diagram: ALLOW angles to the vertical 0—75° Ignore lime water in collecting tube Do not award if additional apparatus used e.g. delivery tube. Do not award if horizontal distance between test tube lips >1cm (1) number Additional guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to the following points:  white and precipitate (forms) (1)  identifies the precipitate as barium carbonate (1) Ignore subsequent tests in (i) and (ii) Allow white solid / crystals Accept formula BaCO If name and formula are given, both must be correct Ignore ammonium chloride (and water) if the precipitate is clearly identified (2) number Additional guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to the following points:  effervescence (precipitate dissolves) (1)  carbon dioxide (is evolved) (1) Accept bubbling / bubbles / fizzing Ignore gas evolves Accept formula CO Ignore barium chloride / BaCl (product) ammonium chloride / NH Cl water / H O (2) (Total for Question 1= 12 marks) number Additional guidance Mark

An answer that makes reference to the following points:  suitable reagent (1)  observation (1) Phosphorus(V) chloride / phosphorus pentachloride / PCl (solid) Allow thionyl chloride / SOCl Do not award PCl / SOCl solution but allow the result mark Steamy fumes / (dense ) white fumes / misty fumes Do not award white smoke Allow add sodium (1) and effervescence / fizzing / bubbles (1) add named carboxylic acid and strong acid catalyst (1) gives fruity smell (1) Do not award acidified dichromate and orange to green (2) number Additional guidance Mark  potassium dichromate(VI) / K Cr O / sodium dichromate(VI) / Na Cr O and sulfuric acid / H SO Allow omission of the oxidation number Just ‘acid / acidified’ Cr O − /H + Ignore heat / reflux / concentrated Do not award Potassium manganate (VII) potassium chromate((VI)) Incorrect oxidation number e.g. potassium dichromate(IV) hydrochloric acid / HCl / Nitric acid / HNO (1) number Additional guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to the following points:  choice of apparatus 1 (1)  the ease of oxidation of the aldehyde (1) Example of a justification: The aldehyde is easily oxidised (to a carboxylic acid) / more easily oxidised than the alcohol Allow To prevent further oxidation Partial oxidation occurs Use of reflux (apparatus 2) results in further oxidation M1 and M2 are standalone (2) number Additional guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to the following points:  suitable reagent (1)  result of the selected test (1) Route 1 (warm with) (blue) Fehling’s / (blue) Benedict’s reagent Red / brown and precipitate / solid Route 2 (warm with) Tollens’ reagent Silver mirror or grey/ black precipitate Ignore Brady’s reagent Do not award potassium dichromate(VI) No observation TE on incorrect reagent (2) number Additional guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to the following points:  The alcohol cannot be identified and because there are two primary alcohols with the molecular formula C H O Accept Alcoho

An answer that makes reference to the following:  results that are within 0.2 cm Allow within 0.1 cm ±0.1 cm of the mean results 0.2 / 0.1 (cm ) apart Do not award ±0.2 cm Ignore Omission of units Reference to ‘good agreement’ / similar values / same values (1) number Additional guidance Mark
 from yellow (1)  to orange (1) Do not award red Correct colours in reverse order scores (1) (2) number Additional guidance Mark

Possible route through the calculation  calculation of amount of hydrochloric acid (1)  calculation of amount of NaOH in 250 cm (1:1 ratio) (1)  calculation of mass of NaOH (1)  calculation of percentage purity of NaOH (1) TE on mean titre from (b) and TE at each stage mol (HCl) = 24.20 x 0.095 x 10 − = 2.299 x 10 − /0.002299 NaOH (in 250) = 10 x 2.299 x 10 − (mol) = 2.299 x 10 − / 0.02299 (mol) Mass NaOH (in 250) = 40 x 2.299 x 10 − = 0.9196 (g) Purity NaOH = 100 x 0.9196 / 0.95 = 96.8% Ignore SF except 1 SF Do not award purity >100% or any value based on an uncalculated mass of NaOH Correct answer no working scores (4) If mean titre calculated using all four titres (24.28 cm ) purity = 97.12% If calculated using all last three titres (24.03 cm ) purity = 96.13% (4) (Total for Question 4 = 8 marks) Total for Paper = 50 marks Pearson Education Limited.
